---
title: "LRP76: Taught vs not-taught expressive vocabulary (generalisation contrast)"

date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

::: {.callout-warning title="Warning: Preliminary model output"}

This model output is preliminary and likely to change as the model evolves and further data are received.

:::

```{python}
# | echo: false
import json
from pathlib import Path

import arviz as az
import pandas as pd

_here = Path(".")
with open(_here / "config.json") as f:
    config = json.load(f)
trace = az.from_netcdf(_here / "trace.nc")
tau_summary = pd.read_csv(_here / "tau_summary.csv")
contrast = pd.read_csv(_here / "tau_contrast_matrix.csv", index_col=0)
difference = pd.read_csv(_here / "tau_difference.csv")
```

## Overview

LRP76 is the within-data test of the trial's "**gains are largest in
directly-taught skills, with little generalisation**" finding. Two Block 1
*expressive* vocabulary outcomes are modelled jointly over the randomised window
(t1 $\rightarrow$ t2):

- `TE` — directly-taught target words (`b1extau`, $N = 24$);
- `UE` — not-taught comparison words (`b1exnt`, $N = 12$).

The headline quantity is the **difference in intervention benefit**,
$\tau_{\mathrm{UE}} - \tau_{\mathrm{TE}}$. Because $\tau$ is the coefficient on the
waiting-control indicator ($G = \text{group} - 1$; group 2 waits for
intervention), a *negative* $\tau$ is a benefit, so $\tau_{\mathrm{UE}} -
\tau_{\mathrm{TE}}$ equals the benefit on **taught** words minus the benefit on
**not-taught** words. A clearly positive difference means the programme raised the
words it *taught* more than untaught words — i.e. limited transfer/generalisation,
consistent with Burgoyne et al. (2012).

This complements the standardised-vocabulary nulls (LRP53/LRP54) and the
per-outcome taught models (LRP74/LRP75): the broader story is that the programme
**teaches what it teaches (expressively), with little transfer** — to untaught
words, to receptive knowledge of the taught words, or to standardised vocabulary.

## Structure

For child $i$ and outcome $k \in \{\mathrm{TE}, \mathrm{UE}\}$:

$$
\eta_{k,i} = \alpha_k + \tau_k G_i
           + \gamma^{\text{own}}_k\, \text{logit}_*(k_{\text{pre},i}, N_k)
           + \sum_{j \neq k} \gamma_{k,j}\, \text{logit}_*(j_{\text{pre},i}, N_j),
$$

with a per-outcome Beta-Binomial likelihood and dispersion $\kappa_k$. The two
outcomes are measured on the **same children** in the same window, so a 2×2 LKJ
residual correlation (`use_residual_correlation`) is included by default to model
the within-child taught/not-taught dependence; this is identifiable here (unlike
the prior-dominated eight-outcome block in LRP55) and keeps the difference
interval from being needlessly inflated.

::: {.callout-note title="Denominators and caveats"}

- Taught ($\mathrm{TE}$): $N = 24$ — documented (Burgoyne et al., 2012, Table 3).
- Not-taught ($\mathrm{UE}$): $N = 12$ — **observed maximum, not documented**. The
  paper tabulates only the 24-item taught tests; the not-taught comparison set's
  item count is inferred from the data (observed max 12, consistent with a
  half-size control set) and flagged unconfirmed. The logit-scale *difference* is
  robust to this, but the probability-scale interpretation of $\mathrm{UE}$ is
  approximate pending the data dictionary.
- $n \approx 54$; the causal claim is limited to the randomised window. The two
  outcomes are on different scales, so the difference is a *differential
  logit-scale effect*, not a difference in raw words.
- Sensitivity: if the residual-correlation block destabilises sampling, set
  `use_residual_correlation=False` (the difference is then conservative — it
  ignores the positive within-child correlation that would tighten it).

:::

![Model diagram](model_graph.svg){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

## Shared priors

![alpha](prior_alpha.svg){width=40%}
![tau](prior_tau.svg){width=40%}
![gamma_own](prior_gamma_own.svg){width=40%}
![gamma_cross](prior_gamma_cross.svg){width=40%}

## Sampling diagnostics

```{python}
# | echo: false
diag = pd.read_csv(_here / "diagnostics.csv", index_col=0)
diag
```

![Trace](trace_plot.png){.lightbox}
![Energy](energy_plot.png){.lightbox}

## Per-outcome treatment effects

::: {.callout-important title="Sign convention: negative τ = intervention benefit"}

Each $\tau_k$ is the coefficient on $G = \text{group} - 1$ (group 2 = waiting
control in the randomised window), so a **negative $\tau_k$ means the intervention
raised outcome $k$** (suite-wide convention). The expectation is
$\tau_{\mathrm{TE}} < 0$ (taught words raised) and $\tau_{\mathrm{UE}}$ closer to
zero (untaught words largely unmoved).

:::

```{python}
# | echo: false
tau_summary
```

## Generalisation contrast — $\tau_{\mathrm{UE}} - \tau_{\mathrm{TE}}$

```{python}
# | echo: false
difference
```

- `diff_logit_*`: posterior mean and equal-tailed interval of $\tau_{\mathrm{UE}} - \tau_{\mathrm{TE}}$ on the logit scale. Since $-\tau$ is the benefit, this is *benefit on taught $-$ benefit on not-taught*; **positive ⇒ taught moved more** (limited generalisation).
- `prob_diff_pos`: $P(\tau_{\mathrm{UE}} > \tau_{\mathrm{TE}})$ — the probability that the directly-taught words moved more than the untaught words.

### Pairwise contrast — $P(\tau_k > \tau_j)$

```{python}
# | echo: false
contrast
```

## Posterior predictive check

![Posterior predictive check](posterior_predictive_check.png){.lightbox}

## LOO-PSIS

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: asis
print("```\n" + open(_here / "loo.txt").read() + "\n```")
```

## Configuration

```{python}
# | echo: false
config
```
